title.en: Synapse & Microsoft Fabric: The Analytics Estate

description.en: Map Azure's analytics landscape: ADLS Gen2 lakes and the medallion pattern, Synapse's pools and distribution strategies, and Microsoft Fabric's OneLake-centered, capacity-priced reimagining of the whole estate.

story_variants.en:
## From Databases to an Estate

Operational stores answer 'what is true right now'. The analytics estate answers 'what has been true, for everyone, joined across every system' — and on Azure that estate has been rebuilt twice in a decade. You will encounter both generations in the wild: **Azure Synapse Analytics** (the mature, still widely deployed platform) and **Microsoft Fabric** (the strategic direction). An enterprise architect needs fluency in both, plus the lake underneath them.

## The Lake: ADLS Gen2 and the Medallion Pattern

Everything starts with **Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2** — blob storage with hierarchical namespace enabled (the storage lesson's checkbox pays off here). The de facto organization is the **medallion architecture**:

- **Bronze**: raw ingested data, immutable, exactly as received.
- **Silver**: cleaned, deduplicated, conformed types, joined reference data.
- **Gold**: business-level aggregates shaped for consumption (star schemas, feature tables).

Open table formats — **Delta Lake** above all, with Parquet underneath — give the lake ACID transactions, time travel, and schema enforcement. This 'lakehouse' pattern is the gravitational center both Synapse and Fabric orbit.

## Synapse: Four Engines in a Trench Coat

Synapse bundles several engines behind one workspace:

- **Dedicated SQL pools** — the former SQL Data Warehouse: provisioned MPP compute measured in DWUs, tables **distributed across 60 distributions**. You choose the strategy per table, and this choice dominates performance:

| Strategy | How | Use for |
|---|---|---|
| **HASH(col)** | Rows placed by hash of a column | Large fact tables; pick a high-cardinality, join-friendly column |
| **ROUND_ROBIN** | Rows dealt evenly, no locality | Staging/landing tables, unknown patterns (the default) |
| **REPLICATE** | Full copy on every compute node | Small dimension tables (rule of thumb: under ~2 GB) |

A fact table hashed on a good key joins to replicated dimensions without data movement; get it wrong and every query shuffles terabytes between nodes (watch for ShuffleMove in the plan — it is the smoking gun).

- **Serverless SQL pool** — pay-per-TB-scanned T-SQL directly over lake files; superb for exploration and logical warehousing without provisioning.
- **Spark pools** — managed Apache Spark for engineering and ML.
- **Pipelines** — Data Factory integration for orchestration and 100+ connectors.

> **Callout:** Serverless-vs-dedicated is an economics question: serverless bills per data scanned (partition and columnar-format your lake!), dedicated bills per provisioned hour (pause it nights and weekends or pay for idle). Many estates run serverless for exploration and a right-sized dedicated pool for the governed gold layer.

## Fabric: The Estate, Reimagined as SaaS

**Microsoft Fabric** (GA since late 2023) rebundles the stack as a single SaaS with two big ideas:

1. **OneLake** — one logical, tenant-wide data lake ('OneDrive for data'), Delta-Parquet native. Every Fabric workload — Lakehouse, Warehouse, Real-Time Intelligence, Data Factory, Power BI, Data Science — reads and writes the *same copy*. **Shortcuts** virtualize external data (ADLS Gen2, S3, Dataverse) into OneLake without copying, and **mirroring** replicates operational databases (Azure SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake) into it continuously.
2. **Capacity pricing** — you buy a pooled capacity (F-SKUs) measured in Capacity Units that all workloads share, with smoothing/bursting to absorb spikes, instead of per-engine meters. Simpler to reason about; also easier for one runaway notebook to exhaust, so capacity monitoring becomes a first-class ops duty.

Power BI's **Direct Lake** mode is the headline trick: semantic models read Delta files in OneLake directly, giving near-import performance without scheduled refresh copies. Governance rides along via **Microsoft Purview** integration — catalog, lineage, sensitivity labels — which is what makes 'estate' more than a metaphor for regulated industries.

> **War story:** A logistics firm ran a proof-of-concept where the same gold-layer Delta tables served Spark feature engineering, a T-SQL warehouse, and Power BI Direct Lake — zero copies. Their previous stack had maintained *five* scheduled copies of the same data with three refresh failure modes. The copies, it turned out, had been most of the operational pain; OneLake's 'one copy, many engines' was the actual product.

## Choosing in 2026

- Heavy existing Synapse estate, stable workloads → keep running it; Microsoft ships migration tooling toward Fabric, and dedicated pools remain supported. Plan the trajectory, don't panic-migrate.
- Greenfield analytics, Power-BI-centric org → Fabric is the default answer.
- Databricks remains the strong third option for Spark-first, multi-cloud, or advanced ML estates — commonly writing Delta to ADLS/OneLake that Fabric then serves to the business. These coexist more than they compete.

## Common Pitfalls

- ROUND_ROBIN fact tables in dedicated pools — the default that quietly costs a fortune in shuffles.
- Forgetting to pause dedicated pools in dev/test — provisioned MPP bills while idle.
- Serverless SQL over millions of tiny un-partitioned JSON files — per-TB-scanned pricing punishes bad lake hygiene.
- Treating Fabric capacity as infinite — one unthrottled notebook can starve the CFO's dashboards.
- Skipping Purview/governance until 'later' — lineage retrofitted onto three years of pipelines is archaeology.

## Mental Model

Think of the estate in three layers: **the lake** (ADLS/OneLake, Delta format — the single source of gravity), **the engines** (SQL, Spark, real-time — increasingly interchangeable visitors to the same data), and **the consumption layer** (Power BI, ML, APIs). Synapse was engines bundled beside a lake; Fabric is engines dissolved into one. Either way, the winning move is identical: keep one governed copy in an open format and let engines come to the data — never the reverse.

description.en: One governed Delta lake at the center; engines visit the data instead of copying it.

left.label: Five scheduled copies of the same data feeding five tools

right.label: One Delta copy in OneLake; Spark, SQL, and Direct Lake all read it

description.en: The copies are the operational pain — 'one copy, many engines' is the actual product of the lakehouse era.

animation_sequence.code:
CREATE TABLE gold.fact_sales
WITH (DISTRIBUTION = HASH(sale_id),
      CLUSTERED COLUMNSTORE INDEX)
AS SELECT ... ;

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_customer
WITH (DISTRIBUTION = REPLICATE)
AS SELECT ... ;

explanation.en: HASH on high-cardinality sale_id spreads the fact table evenly across the 60 distributions.
Small dimensions get REPLICATE — a full copy per node means fact-to-dimension joins need zero data movement.

lines: cause: fact_sales is ROUND_ROBIN; join key not co-located
fix: redistribute as HASH(customer_id) or replicate the dimension

description.en: ShuffleMove in a dedicated pool plan is the smoking gun of a wrong distribution strategy.
The medallion flow from raw file to executive dashboard without a single extra copy.

instruction.en: Encode the Synapse dedicated-pool distribution advisor. For each table dict, apply rules in priority order: (1) type 'dimension' AND rows < 1,000,000 -> 'REPLICATE'; (2) type 'fact' AND a non-empty 'hash_col' present -> 'HASH(<hash_col>)'; (3) otherwise -> 'ROUND_ROBIN'. Implement strategy(table) returning the string, then print one line per table in TABLES order: '<name> -> <strategy>'. This is the exact triage a warehouse review runs on every new table.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
TABLES = [
    {'name': 'dim_customer', 'rows': 500000, 'type': 'dimension'},
    {'name': 'fact_sales', 'rows': 2000000000, 'type': 'fact', 'hash_col': 'sale_id'},
    {'name': 'stg_raw', 'rows': 50000000, 'type': 'staging'},
]

def strategy(table):
    # TODO: apply the three rules in priority order.
    # Rule 2 output format: f"HASH({table['hash_col']})"
    pass

# TODO: loop TABLES and print '<name> -> <strategy>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
dim_customer -> REPLICATE
fact_sales -> HASH(sale_id)
stg_raw -> ROUND_ROBIN

en: Small dimensions replicate: table['type'] == 'dimension' and table['rows'] < 1_000_000.
Use table.get('hash_col') so tables without the key (like stg_raw) fall through safely to ROUND_ROBIN.
The HASH output embeds the column name in parentheses with no spaces: HASH(sale_id).

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert strategy({'name': 'd', 'rows': 500000, 'type': 'dimension'}) == 'REPLICATE'
assert strategy({'name': 'd', 'rows': 5000000, 'type': 'dimension'}) == 'ROUND_ROBIN'
assert strategy({'name': 'f', 'rows': 10, 'type': 'fact', 'hash_col': 'id'}) == 'HASH(id)'
assert strategy({'name': 'f', 'rows': 10, 'type': 'fact'}) == 'ROUND_ROBIN'
assert strategy({'name': 's', 'rows': 1, 'type': 'staging'}) == 'ROUND_ROBIN'

q.en: In a Synapse dedicated SQL pool, why do small dimension tables use REPLICATE distribution?

en: A full copy on every node lets fact joins run with zero data movement
It is required for columnstore indexes

q.en: What is OneLake in Microsoft Fabric?

en: A single logical, tenant-wide Delta-Parquet data lake that every Fabric workload shares
A caching layer for Power BI only

q.en: Power BI Direct Lake mode is notable because it...

en: imports data nightly into a proprietary format
reads Delta files in OneLake directly, avoiding scheduled refresh copies
only works with DirectQuery to SQL Server

q.en: Serverless SQL pool pricing punishes which lake habit most?

en: Millions of tiny un-partitioned files scanned in full per query
Storing data in the same region as compute
